walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Orleans set up. First, unfold the large game board and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach. This board shows the map of medieval France with towns connected by Roads (brown lines) and Waterways (blue lines). There are also character tracks along the edges, supply spaces for goods, and building spaces for neutral Character Tiles. Next, place the smaller Beneficial Deeds board alongside it. This is where players can send followers to do good works for rewards.
walkthrough.image_caption: Large game board and Beneficial Deeds board

walkthrough.title: Place Technology Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 16 Technology Tiles. These are small square tiles that can replace followers on action spaces. Stack them on the appropriate space on the game board. Players will earn these by recruiting Craftsmen throughout the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Technology Tiles stacked on the game board

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Hour Glass Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 18 Hour Glass Tiles. These determine the game's length and events. First, remove the Pilgrimage tile, which has a slightly lighter hue than the others. Shuffle the remaining 17 tiles face down and stack them on the appropriate space on the game board. Then place the Pilgrimage tile face down on top of the stack. The game always starts with the Pilgrimage event in Round 1.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hour Glass Tiles stacked with Pilgrimage on top

walkthrough.title: Place Markers on Tracks
walkthrough.text: Each player takes 7 wooden cubes (markers) in their chosen color. Place 1 marker from each player on the first space of each of the 5 Character Tracks: Farmers, Boatmen, Craftsmen, Traders, and Scholars (also called Knights Track for the Castle). Also place 1 marker on the first space of the Development Track. Each player should have all their markers on starting positions now.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player markers on starting positions of all tracks

walkthrough.title: Sort and Place Neutral Character Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find all the neutral Character Tiles. These are the ones without a colored marking on the back. Sort them by profession: Farmers, Boatmen, Craftsmen, Traders, Knights, Scholars, and Monks. Place each type on the matching building space on the game board. In a 3-player game, remove 2 each of Farmer, Boatman, Craftsman, and Trader tiles, plus 3 each of Knight, Scholar, and Monk tiles. In a 2-player game, remove 4 each of Farmer, Boatman, Craftsman, and Trader tiles, plus 6 each of Knight, Scholar, and Monk tiles.
walkthrough.image_caption: Neutral Character Tiles sorted by profession on the game board

walkthrough.title: Set Up Goods Tiles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all the Goods Tiles face down. Then place them face up on the Road and Waterway spaces on the game board, one tile per space. In a 4-player game, every Road and Waterway space gets a tile. In a 3-player game, leave spaces marked with '4' empty and also remove 6 random Goods Tiles from the game. In a 2-player game, leave spaces marked with '3' or '4' empty and remove 12 random Goods Tiles. Sort all remaining Goods Tiles by type and place them face up on the Goods Market spaces on the left side of the game board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Goods Tiles placed on roads and waterways

walkthrough.title: Prepare Place Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 20 Place Tiles. Sort them into two stacks by the Roman numerals on their reverse side: Stack I and Stack II. Place both stacks on the table where everyone can reach them. Players may look through these stacks at any time during the game. These tiles represent city expansions that players can acquire.
walkthrough.image_caption: Place Tile stacks I and II

walkthrough.title: Place Citizen Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 14 Citizen Tiles. Place 13 of them on the marked spaces on the game board and the Beneficial Deeds board. Put the remaining 1 Citizen Tile on the table nearby. This last tile will go to the player who builds the most Trading Stations at the end of the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Citizen Tiles placed on marked spaces

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Coin Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all 47 coins on the table within easy reach of all players. This forms the general supply. Players will pay coins to and receive coins from this supply throughout the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Coin supply in the center of the table

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes the following in their chosen color: 1 Player Board, 1 Followers Bag (cloth sack), 1 Merchant Token (wooden figure), and 10 Trading Stations (wooden houses). Each player also receives 5 coins from the supply. Place your Trading Stations and coins in front of you.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player components laid out by color

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Followers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes 1 set of starting followers in their player color: 1 Farmer, 1 Boatman, 1 Craftsman, and 1 Trader. These have a colored marking to distinguish them from neutral tiles. Place all 4 of these followers on the Market Spaces of your Player Board. These special followers can never be lost to Plague, used for Beneficial Deeds, or paid during Torture. They will stay with you the entire game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Starting followers placed on the Player Board Market

walkthrough.title: Place Merchant Tokens and Select First Player
walkthrough.text: Each player places their Merchant Token in Orleans, the starting city on the map. The youngest player receives the Start Player Token. You are now ready to play. The game will be played over 18 rounds, each with 7 phases.
walkthrough.image_caption: Merchant Tokens in Orleans, Start Player Token assigned

summary.title: Lay Out the Game Boards
bullets: Place the large game board (map of France with towns, Roads, and Waterways) centrally
Place the smaller Beneficial Deeds board alongside it

summary.title: Place Technology Tiles
bullets: Stack all 16 Technology Tiles on their designated space on the game board
Players earn these by recruiting Craftsmen

summary.title: Prepare the Hour Glass Tiles
bullets: Remove the Pilgrimage tile (lighter hue) from the 18 Hour Glass Tiles
Shuffle the remaining 17 face down and stack them on the game board
Place the Pilgrimage tile face down on top — Round 1 always starts with Pilgrimage

summary.title: Place Markers on Tracks
bullets: Each player takes 7 wooden cubes in their color
Place 1 marker per player on the first space of each of the 5 Character Tracks
Place 1 marker per player on the first space of the Development Track

summary.title: Sort and Place Neutral Character Tiles

Place on matching building spaces on the game board
3-player: remove 2 each of basic 4 types, 3 each of Knight/Scholar/Monk
2-player: remove 4 each of basic 4 types, 6 each of Knight/Scholar/Monk

summary.title: Set Up Goods Tiles
bullets: Shuffle Goods Tiles, place face up on Road and Waterway spaces (1 per space)
4-player: fill every space; 3-player: skip '4' spaces, remove 6 tiles; 2-player: skip '3' and '4' spaces, remove 12 tiles
Sort remaining Goods Tiles by type onto the Goods Market (left side of board)

Place both stacks on the table — players may browse them at any time

summary.title: Set Out the Coin Supply
bullets: Place all 47 coins as a shared supply on the table
Players pay to and receive from this supply throughout the game

summary.title: Hand Out Player Components
bullets: Each player: 1 Player Board, 1 Followers Bag, 1 Merchant Token, 10 Trading Stations
Each player also takes 5 coins from the supply

summary.title: Place Starting Followers
bullets: Each player takes 4 colored starting followers: 1 Farmer, 1 Boatman, 1 Craftsman, 1 Trader
Place all 4 on the Market Spaces of your Player Board
These followers are permanent — cannot be lost to Plague, Beneficial Deeds, or Torture

summary.title: Place Merchant Tokens and Select First Player
bullets: All Merchant Tokens start in Orleans on the map
Youngest player gets the Start Player Token
Game is played over 18 rounds, each with 7 phases

walkthrough.title: Game Structure: 18 Rounds, 7 Phases
walkthrough.text: Orleans is played over exactly 18 rounds. Each round follows the same 7 phases in order: Phase 1 Hour Glass, Phase 2 Census, Phase 3 Followers, Phase 4 Planning, Phase 5 Actions, Phase 6 Event, and Phase 7 Start Player. The Hour Glass Tiles track the rounds. When the 18th tile is drawn, that is the final round. Let's walk through each phase.
walkthrough.image_caption: Overview of the 7-phase round structure

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Hour Glass
walkthrough.text: The Start Player draws the top Hour Glass Tile from the stack and flips it face up. This tile shows an event that will affect the current round. There are 6 types of events: Pilgrimage (3 tiles), Income (3), Harvest (3), Taxes (3), Trading Day (3), and Plague (3). Most events resolve later in Phase 6, but Pilgrimage takes effect during Phase 5 Actions instead. The events add tension and force players to adapt.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hour Glass Tile revealing the round's event

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Census
walkthrough.text: Now check the Farmers Track. The player whose marker is furthest ahead receives 1 coin from the supply. If there is a tie for first, nobody receives the coin. The player whose marker is furthest behind must pay 1 coin to the supply. If there is a tie for last, nobody pays. In a 2-player game, the player who is behind does not pay, but the player ahead still receives a coin. This phase rewards investing in Farmers early.
walkthrough.image_caption: Farmers Track showing leader and trailing positions

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Followers
walkthrough.text: Each player draws Character Tiles from their Followers Bag and places them on their Market (the spaces on their Player Board). You may draw up to the number shown by your position on the Knights Track. At the start of the game, this is 4. You cannot draw more tiles than you have empty Market spaces. Tiles left on your Market from previous rounds still take up space, so plan ahead. Here is a special rule: for each follower you decline to draw, you may move one Character Tile from an Action Space back to your Market. This lets you rearrange planned actions.
walkthrough.image_caption: Drawing followers from the bag to the Market

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Planning
walkthrough.text: Now comes the heart of the game. Take Character Tiles from your Market and place them on the Action Spaces of the Places you want to activate. Each Place requires specific types of followers. For example, the Farm House needs a Boatman and a Craftsman. A Place is only activated once ALL of its required Action Spaces are filled. You do not have to fill all spaces in one round. You can place some tiles now and finish in a future round. Partially filled Places stay as-is between rounds. All players plan simultaneously, then announce when done. If order matters, resolve in clockwise order from the Start Player.
walkthrough.image_caption: Placing followers on Action Spaces to activate Places

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Actions
walkthrough.text: Beginning with the Start Player and going clockwise, each player may carry out 1 activated action or pass. After performing an action, remove the Character Tiles from that Place and return them to your Followers Bag. Technology Tiles remain permanently. If you pass, you are done for this phase and cannot take more actions. You may carry out actions in any order, and you are not required to use an activated action. Unplayed activated actions carry over to the next round with their tiles still in place. The phase ends when all players have passed.
walkthrough.image_caption: Taking turns carrying out activated actions

walkthrough.title: The Farm House
walkthrough.text: The Farm House requires a Boatman and a Craftsman. When activated, take a Farmer tile from the game board and put it into your bag. Then advance your marker one space on the Farmers Track and receive the good depicted on that space. Your Farmers Track position also determines who gains or pays during the Census phase. Each good type has a different point value at game end: Grain (1), Cheese (2), Wine (3), Wool (4), and Brocade (5).
walkthrough.image_caption: Farm House action: gain a Farmer and advance on the track

walkthrough.title: The Village: Boatman, Craftsman, or Trader
walkthrough.text: The Village requires a Farmer and a Boatman. When activated, you choose ONE of three sub-actions. Boatman: Take a Boatman tile, put it in your bag, advance on the Boatmen Track, and receive the depicted coins. The last space gives a Citizen Tile instead of coins (first player there only). Craftsman: Take a Craftsman tile, put it in your bag, advance on the Craftsmen Track, and receive a Technology Tile. Trader: Take a Trader tile, put it in your bag, advance on the Traders Track, and gain a Place Tile to expand your city.
walkthrough.image_caption: Village offering three recruitment options

walkthrough.title: Technology Tiles (from Craftsman)
walkthrough.text: When you gain a Technology Tile, you may only use it after you pass during the current Action Phase. Place it on any Action Space of your choice to permanently replace the required Character Tile there. Important constraints: your first Technology Tile must replace a Farmer. Subsequent tiles may replace any type except Monks. You may not place more than 1 Technology Tile at one Place. You may not place a Technology Tile at a Place requiring only a single Character Tile. Technology Tiles cannot be moved once placed. They remain for the entire game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Technology Tile permanently filling an Action Space

walkthrough.title: Expanding the City (from Trader)
walkthrough.text: When you advance on the Traders Track, you gain a Place Tile to expand your city. There are two stacks: I and II. On your first advance, you may only choose from Stack I. On every subsequent advance, you may choose from Stack I or II. Place the tile next to your Player Board. This gives you a new action you can activate in future rounds. Place Tiles include production buildings (Hayrick, Cheese Factory, Winery, Wool Manufacturer, Tailor Shop), coin generators (Brewery, Cellar, Office), development buildings (Shipping Line, Library, Windmill, Pharmacy), and special abilities (Bathhouse, School, Herb Garden, Sacristy, Gunpowder Tower, Laboratory, Hospital, Horse Wagon).
walkthrough.image_caption: Expanding your city with a new Place Tile

walkthrough.title: The University
walkthrough.text: The University requires a Farmer, a Craftsman, and a Trader. When activated, take a Scholar tile from the game board and put it into your bag. Then advance one space on the Scholars Track and receive the depicted number of Development Points. For each Development Point, advance your marker on the Development Track. Your Development Status multiplies the value of your Trading Stations and Citizen Tiles at game end, so this is a powerful long-term investment.
walkthrough.image_caption: University action: gain a Scholar and Development Points

walkthrough.title: The Castle
walkthrough.text: The Castle requires a Farmer, a Boatman, and a Trader. When activated, take a Knight tile from the game board and put it into your bag. Then advance one space on the Knights Track. The Knights Track determines how many followers you may draw from your bag each round during Phase 3. At the start, you draw 4. Recruiting Knights increases this up to 8. When you reach the fourth Knight position (7 draws), you do not increase further but the first player there receives a Citizen Tile.
walkthrough.image_caption: Castle action: gain a Knight and increase draw limit

walkthrough.title: The Monastery
walkthrough.text: The Monastery requires a Farmer, a Craftsman, a Boatman, and a Trader. When activated, take a Monk tile from the game board and put it into your bag. There is no track bonus. However, Monks are wildcards. They can be used in place of any other Character Tile type when planning actions. The reverse is not true: you cannot use other Character Tiles or Technology Tiles in place of a Monk. This makes Monks extremely flexible and valuable.
walkthrough.image_caption: Monastery action: gain a versatile Monk

walkthrough.title: The Ship and Wagon
walkthrough.text: The Ship requires a Boatman, a Craftsman, and a Knight. When activated, move your Merchant Token from its current town along a Waterway (blue connection) to an adjacent town. If there is a Goods Tile on the route, you may take one of them. The Wagon works the same way but uses Roads (brown connections) instead of Waterways. The Wagon requires a Farmer, a Knight, and a Scholar. You may use empty Roads or Waterways; you just do not collect a good.
walkthrough.image_caption: Moving the Merchant along Waterways or Roads to collect goods

walkthrough.title: The Guildhall
walkthrough.text: The Guildhall requires a Craftsman, a Trader, and a Knight. When activated, build a Trading Station in the town where your Merchant Token is currently located. Each town can only have one Trading Station, regardless of which player built it. Exception: in Orleans, every player may build one Trading Station. Trading Stations are worth VP equal to your Development Status at game end, plus the player with the most Trading Stations receives a bonus Citizen Tile.
walkthrough.image_caption: Building a Trading Station with the Guildhall

walkthrough.title: The Scriptorium
walkthrough.text: The Scriptorium requires a Scholar and a Knight. When activated, you receive 1 Development Point. Advance your marker one space on the Development Track. This is a simpler way to gain development compared to the University.
walkthrough.image_caption: Scriptorium action: gain 1 Development Point

walkthrough.title: The Town Hall and Beneficial Deeds
walkthrough.text: The Town Hall is unique. During the Planning Phase, you can place 1 or 2 Character Tiles in the Town Hall. It does not require both spaces to activate. When you use the Town Hall action, move one or both tiles to free appropriate spaces on the Beneficial Deeds board. You receive 1, 2, or 3 coins per character placed, depending on the space. When you complete a Beneficial Deed by placing the last tile, you receive that deed's Citizen Tile. Important: you must use the exact Character Tiles shown on the spaces, not substitutes like Monks. Also, you may not place your starting colored followers in the Town Hall.
walkthrough.image_caption: Town Hall action: sending followers to Beneficial Deeds

walkthrough.title: The Development Track
walkthrough.text: The Development Track is critical for scoring. Some spaces show coins, which you receive when moving onto or past them. Some spaces have Citizen Tiles, which go to the first player to reach or pass them. Development Status Spaces show a value (starting at 1) that becomes your Development Status when you reach them. Your Development Status determines: the coins you receive from the Income event, the coins from the Hospital Place Tile, and most importantly, the VP value of each Trading Station and Citizen Tile at game end.
walkthrough.image_caption: Development Track with coins, Citizen Tiles, and Status spaces

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: Event
walkthrough.text: Now resolve the event on this round's Hour Glass Tile. Pilgrimage: Already handled during Phase 5 — Monks cannot be recruited this round. Income: Receive coins equal to your Development Status. Harvest: Pay 1 food item (Grain, Cheese, or Wine). If you cannot, pay 5 coins. If you cannot do that, undergo Torture. Taxes: Pay 1 coin per 3 goods you own. If you cannot, undergo Torture. Trading Day: Receive 1 coin per Trading Station you have built. Plague: Draw a random Character Tile from your bag and return it to the game board. If you draw one of your starting colored followers, you are lucky and lose nothing.
walkthrough.image_caption: Resolving the round's event

walkthrough.title: Torture (Bankruptcy)
walkthrough.text: If you cannot pay a required cost, you must undergo Torture. For each missing coin, you must sacrifice one item from this list: a Trading Station (built or from supply), a Follower (drawn randomly from your bag, but your colored starting followers are exempt), a Development Point (move backward, but not onto or past a coin space), a Goods Tile, a Place Tile, or a Technology Tile. You can mix and match. All items lost to Torture are removed from the game permanently. Only Development Points can be re-earned.
walkthrough.image_caption: Torture: sacrificing items to cover unpaid debts

walkthrough.title: Phase 7: Start Player
walkthrough.text: The current Start Player passes the Start Player Token to the player on their left. A new round begins. This continues for 18 rounds.
walkthrough.image_caption: Passing the Start Player Token

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the 18th round. Before scoring, the player with the most Trading Stations receives the remaining Citizen Tile. If tied, no one gets it. Then calculate victory points: Coins are worth 1 VP each. Goods are worth their printed value (Grain 1, Cheese 2, Wine 3, Wool 4, Brocade 5). Each Trading Station and each Citizen Tile is worth VP equal to your Development Status. For example, 5 Trading Stations and 2 Citizen Tiles with Development Status 4 equals (5+2) x 4 = 28 VP. Highest total wins. Tiebreaker: furthest ahead on the Development Track. If still tied, share the victory.
walkthrough.image_caption: Final scoring: coins, goods, Trading Stations, Citizen Tiles

summary.title: Game Structure: 18 Rounds, 7 Phases
bullets: Game = 18 rounds, each with 7 phases in order
Phases: Hour Glass, Census, Followers, Planning, Actions, Event, Start Player
Game ends when the 18th Hour Glass Tile is drawn

summary.title: Phase 1: Hour Glass
bullets: Start Player draws top Hour Glass Tile and reveals it
6 event types (3 tiles each): Pilgrimage, Income, Harvest, Taxes, Trading Day, Plague
Most events resolve in Phase 6; Pilgrimage resolves in Phase 5

summary.title: Phase 2: Census
bullets: Check Farmers Track: leader gets 1 coin, trailer pays 1 coin
Ties cancel the reward/penalty
2-player variant: trailing player does not pay

summary.title: Phase 3: Followers
bullets: Draw Character Tiles from bag up to your Knights Track number (starts at 4)
Place drawn tiles on your Market; cannot exceed empty Market spaces
For each draw declined, may move 1 tile from an Action Space back to Market

summary.title: Phase 4: Planning
bullets: Place tiles from Market onto Action Spaces to activate Places
Each Place requires specific follower types; activated when all spaces filled
Can spread placement across multiple rounds; partial placements persist
All players plan simultaneously

summary.title: Phase 5: Actions
bullets: Starting from Start Player, clockwise: each player takes 1 action or passes
After an action, return Character Tiles to bag (Technology Tiles stay)
Passing ends your participation in this phase
Unused activated actions carry over to next round

summary.title: The Farm House
bullets: Requires: Boatman + Craftsman
Gain a Farmer tile, advance on Farmers Track, receive depicted good
Goods values: Grain (1 VP), Cheese (2), Wine (3), Wool (4), Brocade (5)
Farmers Track position affects Census income/penalty

summary.title: The Village: Boatman, Craftsman, or Trader
bullets: Requires: Farmer + Boatman; choose 1 of 3 sub-actions
Boatman: gain Boatman, advance track, receive coins (or Citizen Tile at end)
Craftsman: gain Craftsman, advance track, receive Technology Tile
Trader: gain Trader, advance track, gain a Place Tile (city expansion)

summary.title: Technology Tiles (from Craftsman)
bullets: Place after passing to permanently fill an Action Space
First tile must replace a Farmer; subsequent tiles replace any type except Monks
Max 1 Technology Tile per Place; cannot be placed on single-tile Places
Cannot be moved once placed

summary.title: Expanding the City (from Trader)
bullets: First advance: choose from Stack I only
Subsequent advances: choose from Stack I or II
Place Tiles add new actions: production, coins, development, special abilities
Players may browse Place Tile stacks at any time

summary.title: The University
bullets: Requires: Farmer + Craftsman + Trader
Gain a Scholar tile, advance on Scholars Track, receive Development Points
Development Points advance your marker on the Development Track
Development Status multiplies Trading Station and Citizen Tile VP at game end

summary.title: The Castle
bullets: Requires: Farmer + Boatman + Trader
Gain a Knight tile, advance on Knights Track
Knights Track increases your follower draw limit (starts at 4, up to 8)
Fourth Knight position: first player there gets a Citizen Tile

summary.title: The Monastery
bullets: Requires: Farmer + Craftsman + Boatman + Trader (all 4 types)
Gain a Monk tile — no track bonus
Monks are wildcards: can substitute for any other Character Tile type
Other tiles and Technology cannot substitute for Monks

summary.title: The Ship and Wagon
bullets: Ship (Boatman + Craftsman + Knight): move Merchant along a Waterway, collect a good
Wagon (Farmer + Knight + Scholar): move Merchant along a Road, collect a good
May travel on empty routes — just no good collected
Can only collect 1 good per movement

summary.title: The Guildhall
bullets: Requires: Craftsman + Trader + Knight
Build a Trading Station in your Merchant's current town
One Trading Station per town (exception: Orleans allows one per player)
Trading Stations = VP equal to Development Status at end of game

summary.title: The Scriptorium
bullets: Requires: Scholar + Knight
Gain 1 Development Point (advance on Development Track)
Simpler development option compared to the University

summary.title: The Town Hall and Beneficial Deeds
bullets: Place 1-2 Character Tiles in Town Hall during Planning; does not need all spaces filled
Action: move tiles to Beneficial Deeds board, receive 1-3 coins per tile placed
Complete a Deed (fill all spaces) to earn that Deed's Citizen Tile
Must use exact Character Tile types shown; no Monks, no colored starting followers

summary.title: The Development Track
bullets: Coin spaces: receive coins when reaching or passing them
Citizen Tile spaces: first player to reach or pass gets the tile
Status spaces: set your Development Status value (starts at 1)
Development Status affects Income event, Hospital, and end-game VP multiplier

summary.title: Phase 6: Event
bullets: Pilgrimage: no Monk recruitment this round (already enforced in Phase 5)
Income: gain coins = Development Status; Harvest: pay 1 food or 5 coins
Taxes: pay 1 coin per 3 goods; Trading Day: gain 1 coin per Trading Station
Plague: draw random follower from bag, return to board (colored followers = safe)

summary.title: Torture (Bankruptcy)
bullets: Triggered when you cannot pay a required cost
Each missing coin replaced by: Trading Station, Follower, Development Point, Goods, Place Tile, or Technology Tile
All sacrificed items removed from game permanently (except Development Points can be re-earned)
Starting colored followers are exempt from Torture

summary.title: Phase 7: Start Player
bullets: Pass Start Player Token to the left
Begin a new round with Phase 1

summary.title: End of Game and Scoring
bullets: Game ends after Round 18; most Trading Stations earns bonus Citizen Tile (tie = no one)
Coins: 1 VP each; Goods: Grain 1, Cheese 2, Wine 3, Wool 4, Brocade 5
Trading Stations + Citizen Tiles: each worth VP = Development Status
Tiebreaker: furthest on Development Track; if still tied, shared victory

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Orleans
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with 4 example players — Player 1 (Red), Player 2 (Blue), Player 3 (Green), and Player 4 (Yellow). Each player starts with 5 coins, 4 starting followers on their Market (Farmer, Boatman, Craftsman, Trader), and their Merchant Token in Orleans. Follow along to see how the phases work, even if you have fewer players at your table.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four players ready for Round 1 in Orleans

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Hour Glass — Pilgrimage
walkthrough.text: Red is the youngest, so they are the Start Player. Red draws the top Hour Glass Tile — it is Pilgrimage, as always in Round 1. This means no player may recruit Monks this round. The Monastery action is off-limits. Everyone nods and moves on.
walkthrough.image_caption: Pilgrimage tile revealed for Round 1

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Census
walkthrough.text: Everyone checks the Farmers Track. All markers are on the first space — a four-way tie. Since ties cancel both the reward and the penalty, nobody gains or pays a coin. Census will matter more once players start recruiting Farmers and pulling ahead on the track.
walkthrough.image_caption: All markers tied on the Farmers Track

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Followers
walkthrough.text: Each player may draw up to 4 followers from their bag (Knights Track starting position). But wait — this is Round 1. Everyone's bag is empty because all 4 starting followers are already on the Market from setup. So there is nothing to draw. In future rounds, after followers are returned to the bag from completed actions, you will draw them out again here. For now, everyone proceeds with their 4 starting followers.
walkthrough.image_caption: Bags are empty — starting followers already on Markets

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Planning — Red Plans the Farm House
walkthrough.text: Time to plan. Red looks at the Farm House, which needs a Boatman and a Craftsman. Red has both on the Market. Red places the Boatman and Craftsman on the Farm House Action Spaces. The Farm House is now activated. Red still has a Farmer and Trader left on the Market, but decides to save them for now.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red activates the Farm House with Boatman and Craftsman

walkthrough.title: Planning — Blue Plans the Village
walkthrough.text: Blue decides to recruit a new follower. The Village needs a Farmer and a Boatman. Blue places both on the Village Action Spaces. The Village is activated. Blue plans to choose the Craftsman option to gain a Technology Tile for long-term efficiency. Blue has a Craftsman and Trader remaining on the Market.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue activates the Village with Farmer and Boatman

walkthrough.title: Planning — Green Plans the Village Too
walkthrough.text: Green also activates the Village with Farmer and Boatman, planning to choose the Trader option to gain a Place Tile and expand their city. Green has Craftsman and Trader left on the Market.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green activates the Village

walkthrough.title: Planning — Yellow Makes a Mistake
walkthrough.text: Yellow looks at the Monastery and says, 'I want a Monk — they can substitute for anything!' Yellow tries to place all 4 followers (Farmer, Boatman, Craftsman, Trader) on the Monastery. But another player points out: this round's event is Pilgrimage, which means no one can recruit Monks! Yellow has wasted their planning because they did not pay attention to the Hour Glass Tile.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yellow caught trying to recruit a Monk during Pilgrimage

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check the Event
walkthrough.text: The takeaway: always look at the Hour Glass Tile before you plan. Pilgrimage blocks the Monastery. Harvest means you need a food item ready. Plague means you might lose a follower from your bag. Plan around the event, not against it. Yellow removes the followers from the Monastery and instead activates the Farm House with Boatman and Craftsman, keeping the Farmer and Trader on the Market.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yellow rethinks and activates the Farm House instead

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Actions — Red Uses the Farm House
walkthrough.text: Starting with Red, each player takes one action at a time. Red uses the activated Farm House: takes a Farmer tile from the game board, puts it into the Followers Bag, advances the marker one space on the Farmers Track, and collects the depicted good — a Grain tile. Red returns the Boatman and Craftsman to the bag. Good start — Red now has a Farmer advantage for Census.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red gains a Farmer, advances on the track, collects Grain

walkthrough.title: Actions — Blue Uses the Village (Craftsman)
walkthrough.text: Blue's turn. Blue activates the Village and chooses the Craftsman option. Blue takes a Craftsman tile from the game board, puts it in the bag, advances on the Craftsmen Track, and receives a Technology Tile. Blue sets the Technology Tile aside — it can only be placed after Blue passes this round. The Farmer and Boatman go back into the bag.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue gains a Craftsman and a Technology Tile

walkthrough.title: Actions — Green Uses the Village (Trader)
walkthrough.text: Green activates the Village and chooses the Trader option. Green takes a Trader tile, puts it in the bag, advances on the Traders Track, and picks a Place Tile from Stack I. Green browses the options and picks the Brewery (receive 2 coins when activated). A solid early economy choice. The Farmer and Boatman go back into the bag.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green gains a Trader and the Brewery Place Tile

walkthrough.title: Actions — Yellow Uses the Farm House
walkthrough.text: Yellow uses the Farm House, taking a Farmer tile, advancing on the Farmers Track, and collecting a Grain tile. The Boatman and Craftsman go back into the bag. Yellow has recovered from the Monastery mistake.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yellow gains a Farmer and Grain

walkthrough.title: Remaining Actions — Players Pass
walkthrough.text: Back to Red. Red still has a Farmer and Trader on the Market but no activated actions remaining. Red passes. Blue passes but first places the Technology Tile — the first Technology Tile must replace a Farmer on an Action Space. Blue places it on the Farm House's Boatman space... wait, the first Technology Tile must replace a Farmer, not a Boatman! Blue corrects the mistake and places it on a space that requires a Farmer instead. Green and Yellow also pass.
walkthrough.image_caption: Players pass; Blue places their Technology Tile correctly

walkthrough.title: Yellow's Second Mistake: Bag Confusion
walkthrough.text: As Yellow puts followers back into the bag, Yellow accidentally puts the colored starting Trader (which was still on the Market, unused) into the bag along with the returned Boatman and Craftsman. Another player notices and reminds Yellow: unused followers on your Market stay on your Market between rounds. Only followers removed from Action Spaces go back into the bag. Yellow retrieves the Trader from the bag and places it back on the Market.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yellow corrects a bag mistake — Market tiles stay on Market

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Market vs. Bag
walkthrough.text: The key distinction: followers only go into your bag after you complete an action and remove them from Action Spaces. Tiles sitting on your Market at the end of the round stay there. They will take up Market space next round, limiting how many new tiles you can draw. This is an important concept — keeping your Market clear gives you more flexibility in future rounds.
walkthrough.image_caption: Market tiles persist; returned action tiles go to bag

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: Event — Pilgrimage (Already Resolved)
walkthrough.text: The Pilgrimage event was already enforced during Phase 5 — no Monks were recruited. There is nothing further to resolve. In future rounds, events like Harvest, Taxes, or Plague will demand attention here. Always plan for the event before it hits.
walkthrough.image_caption: Pilgrimage already handled — no further event resolution

walkthrough.title: Phase 7: Start Player Passes
walkthrough.text: Red passes the Start Player Token to Blue (the player on the left). Round 1 is complete. Let's review what happened: Red and Yellow gained Farmers and Grain. Blue gained a Craftsman and Technology Tile. Green gained a Trader and the Brewery Place Tile. Each player used 2 of their 4 starting followers for actions and kept 2 on the Market.
walkthrough.image_caption: Start Player Token passes to Blue for Round 2

walkthrough.title: Round 2 Preview
walkthrough.text: In Round 2, players will draw followers from their bags during Phase 3. Red will draw the Boatman, Craftsman, and new Farmer (3 tiles). Blue will draw the Farmer, Boatman, and new Craftsman (3 tiles). Players who still have tiles on the Market from Round 1 have less room for draws. This is the rhythm of Orleans: recruit followers, plan actions, execute, return followers to bag, draw them again. Your bag grows each round as you recruit more characters.
walkthrough.image_caption: Looking ahead to Round 2 with growing follower bags

walkthrough.title: Yellow's Third Mistake: Overloading the Bag
walkthrough.text: Looking at the broader game, Yellow realizes a problem. In Round 1 they recruited a Farmer — good. But they already have the starting 4 followers plus the new Farmer. With only 4 draws per round and 5 followers, Yellow will not always draw what they need. If Yellow keeps recruiting followers without increasing the Knights Track draw limit, the bag will fill up with tiles that may not come out when needed. This is called bag bloat.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yellow realizes their bag is getting crowded

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Bag
walkthrough.text: In Orleans, your Followers Bag is like a deck in a deckbuilder. Adding followers is powerful, but only if you can draw them when you need them. Investing in Knights (to increase draw limit) or Technology Tiles (to permanently fill Action Spaces so you need fewer draws) keeps your engine efficient. Adding followers without increasing draw capacity leads to frustration. Think of Knights and Technology as the lubricant that keeps your engine running smoothly.
walkthrough.image_caption: Balance bag growth with draw limit and Technology

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen all 7 phases of a full round, watched Yellow learn from three common mistakes — trying to recruit during Pilgrimage, confusing Market and bag, and not managing bag efficiency — and seen how different strategies begin to form. Red and Yellow went for Farmers and goods. Blue invested in Technology. Green expanded their city. Each path leads to victory points in different ways.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round 1 complete — four different strategies emerging

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into your full 18-round game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for beginner strategy advice on follower priorities, bag management, and when to build Trading Stations.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every action, event, Place Tile, and game term.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building your medieval empire!

summary.title: Welcome to Orleans
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 players (Red, Blue, Green, Yellow)
Each starts with 5 coins, 4 starting followers on Market, Merchant in Orleans
Covers a complete Round 1 with all 7 phases

summary.title: Phase 1: Hour Glass — Pilgrimage
bullets: Round 1 always starts with Pilgrimage
No Monk recruitment allowed this round
Players must plan around the event

bullets: All markers tied on Farmers Track — no coins gained or paid
Census matters more as players diverge on the Farmers Track

bullets: Round 1: bags are empty, starting followers already on Market
In future rounds, you draw returned followers from your bag here
Draw limit starts at 4 (Knights Track)

summary.title: Phase 4: Planning — Red Plans the Farm House
bullets: Red places Boatman + Craftsman on Farm House — it is activated
Farmer and Trader remain on Market for future use

bullets: Blue places Farmer + Boatman on Village — activated
Blue plans to choose the Craftsman option for a Technology Tile

summary.title: Planning — Green Plans the Village Too
bullets: Green activates Village with Farmer + Boatman
Green plans to choose the Trader option for a Place Tile

summary.title: Planning — Yellow Makes a Mistake
bullets: Yellow tries to activate the Monastery for a Monk
Pilgrimage event blocks Monk recruitment this round
Planning wasted because Yellow ignored the Hour Glass Tile

summary.title: Lesson: Always Check the Event
bullets: Always read the Hour Glass Tile before planning
Pilgrimage blocks Monastery; Harvest demands food; Plague costs a follower
Yellow corrects course and activates the Farm House instead

summary.title: Phase 5: Actions — Red Uses the Farm House
bullets: Red gains a Farmer tile, advances on Farmers Track, collects Grain
Boatman and Craftsman return to Red's bag
Red now leads the Farmers Track for Census advantage

summary.title: Actions — Blue Uses the Village (Craftsman)
bullets: Blue gains a Craftsman tile and a Technology Tile
Technology Tile can be placed after Blue passes
First Technology Tile must replace a Farmer on an Action Space

summary.title: Actions — Green Uses the Village (Trader)
bullets: Green gains a Trader tile and picks the Brewery from Stack I
Brewery provides 2 coins per activation — good early economy
First Traders Track advance: Stack I only

summary.title: Actions — Yellow Uses the Farm House
bullets: Yellow gains a Farmer and Grain after recovering from the Monastery mistake
Boatman and Craftsman return to bag

summary.title: Remaining Actions — Players Pass
bullets: Players with no more activated actions pass
Blue places Technology Tile — must replace a Farmer (first tile rule)
Action Phase ends when all players have passed

summary.title: Yellow's Second Mistake: Bag Confusion
bullets: Yellow accidentally puts an unused Market tile into the bag
Correction: only tiles removed from Action Spaces go back in the bag
Unused Market tiles stay on Market between rounds

summary.title: Lesson: Market vs. Bag
bullets: Followers go to bag only after completing an action
Market tiles persist between rounds and take up draw space
Clear Market = more room for new draws next round

summary.title: Phase 6: Event — Pilgrimage (Already Resolved)
bullets: Pilgrimage was enforced during Phase 5 — nothing more to resolve
Other events (Harvest, Taxes, Plague) resolve here in future rounds

summary.title: Phase 7: Start Player Passes
bullets: Red passes Start Player Token to Blue
Round 1 complete: players gained different followers and resources
Each player used 2 of 4 starting followers; 2 remain on Market

summary.title: Round 2 Preview
bullets: Phase 3 now matters: players draw returned followers from bags
Bags are growing as new followers are recruited
Core rhythm: recruit, plan, execute, return to bag, draw again

summary.title: Yellow's Third Mistake: Overloading the Bag
bullets: Yellow has 5 followers in bag/Market but only draws 4 per round
Adding followers without increasing draw limit causes 'bag bloat'
Needed followers may not come out of the bag when needed

summary.title: Lesson: Manage Your Bag
bullets: Bag works like a deck — more tiles = less predictable draws
Knights increase draw limit; Technology Tiles reduce draw needs
Balance bag growth with draw efficiency for a smooth engine

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have seen all 7 phases of a full round
Yellow learned from 3 mistakes: ignoring events, bag confusion, bag bloat
Four strategies emerged: Farmers, Technology, city expansion, goods

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips for beginner strategy advice
Reference the Appendix for every action, event, and game term
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Knights Early
walkthrough.text: Your draw limit starts at 4 followers per round. That is barely enough to activate one Place. By recruiting even one Knight early, you increase your draw to 5, which opens up the ability to activate two Places in a single round. A second Knight bumps you to 6 draws. The difference between drawing 4 and drawing 6 followers is enormous — it is the difference between executing one action and executing two or three. Prioritize the Castle early to keep your engine running smoothly as your bag grows.

walkthrough.title: Get a Technology Tile in Round 1 or 2
walkthrough.text: Technology Tiles are one of the most efficient upgrades in the game. A single Technology Tile permanently fills one Action Space, meaning you need to draw one fewer follower to activate that Place. Your first Technology Tile must replace a Farmer. Place it on your most-used action — the Farm House or Village are ideal targets. This effectively gives you a free draw slot every round for the rest of the game. Combine Technology Tiles with Knights for maximum efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Balance Bag Growth with Draw Capacity
walkthrough.text: This is the most common beginner trap in Orleans. New players recruit lots of followers but never increase their draw limit or add Technology Tiles. The result is a bag stuffed with tiles they cannot draw. Think of your bag like a deck in a deckbuilder game. Every follower you add makes the bag less predictable. Counter this by either recruiting Knights (more draws) or Craftsmen for Technology Tiles (fewer tiles needed). A lean, efficient bag beats a bloated one every time.

walkthrough.title: Plan Around Events
walkthrough.text: Every Hour Glass Tile creates pressure. Harvest forces you to pay a food item — always keep at least one Grain, Cheese, or Wine in reserve. Taxes penalize you for holding too many goods — sometimes it is better to spend goods before Taxes hits. Plague removes a random follower from your bag — a bigger bag means less impact. Trading Day rewards Trading Stations — build them before this event comes up. Income scales with Development Status — push development early to maximize payouts. Check the Hour Glass Tile every round and plan accordingly.

walkthrough.title: The Development Track is a Multiplier
walkthrough.text: Development Status is the single most impactful scoring multiplier in the game. It multiplies the value of every Trading Station and every Citizen Tile you own. A player with Development Status 4 and 7 Trading Stations plus Citizen Tiles scores 28 VP from that alone. A player with the same Trading Stations but Development Status 2 scores only 14. Double the points for the same number of buildings. Invest in Scholars and the University early so your Development Status grows alongside your Trading Station network.

walkthrough.title: Beneficial Deeds Are Worth Doing
walkthrough.text: New players often ignore the Town Hall and Beneficial Deeds board because sending followers away permanently feels expensive. But the rewards are significant: coins immediately, plus a Citizen Tile for completing a Deed. Citizen Tiles are multiplied by your Development Status at game end. If you complete even one Deed and have Development Status 3, that single Citizen Tile is worth 3 VP plus the coins you earned along the way. Start contributing early when you have spare followers that are clogging your bag.

walkthrough.title: Goods Values Are Not Equal
walkthrough.text: Not all goods are created equal. Brocade is worth 5 VP, while Grain is only 1 VP. When moving your Merchant along routes, prioritize paths with high-value goods tiles. When the Harvest event comes, always pay Grain first since it is the cheapest to lose. When collecting goods from the Farmers Track, note that advancing further yields better goods. The Goods Market refills from returned Harvest payments and Plague returns, so valuable goods can cycle back into play.

walkthrough.title: Build Trading Stations in Clusters
walkthrough.text: Moving your Merchant requires the Ship or Wagon action, which costs 3 followers each time. That is expensive. Plan your movement route so you can build Trading Stations in clusters along connected towns rather than zigzagging across the map. Each Trading Station scores VP equal to your Development Status at game end, and the player with the most stations gets a bonus Citizen Tile. A focused route of 4-5 stations near each other is much more efficient than 2-3 scattered across the map.

summary.title: Prioritize Knights Early
bullets: Draw limit starts at 4 — barely enough for 1 action per round
Each Knight increases draws (up to 8): huge engine improvement
More draws = ability to activate multiple Places per round
Target the Castle in Rounds 1-3

summary.title: Get a Technology Tile in Round 1 or 2
bullets: Technology permanently fills 1 Action Space — 1 fewer draw needed
First tile must replace a Farmer; place on Farm House or Village
Combines with Knights for massive efficiency gains
Recruit a Craftsman via the Village early

summary.title: Balance Bag Growth with Draw Capacity
bullets: Adding followers without more draws = bag bloat
Counter with Knights (more draws) or Technology (fewer tiles needed)
Think of your bag like a deckbuilder — lean and efficient beats bloated
Most common beginner trap in Orleans

summary.title: Plan Around Events
bullets: Harvest: keep at least 1 food item in reserve (Grain/Cheese/Wine)
Taxes: 1 coin per 3 goods — spend goods before Taxes if possible
Plague: random follower lost — bigger bag = less impact per loss
Trading Day and Income reward Trading Stations and Development respectively

summary.title: The Development Track is a Multiplier
bullets: Development Status multiplies value of ALL Trading Stations and Citizen Tiles
Status 4 with 7 items = 28 VP; Status 2 with same items = only 14 VP
Invest in Scholars/University early to grow Status alongside stations
Development Track also gives coins and Citizen Tiles along the way

summary.title: Beneficial Deeds Are Worth Doing
bullets: Sending followers to Deeds earns immediate coins + Citizen Tiles on completion
Citizen Tiles multiplied by Development Status at game end
Also thins your bag — fewer followers to draw through
Start contributing early with spare or duplicate followers

summary.title: Goods Values Are Not Equal
bullets: Brocade = 5 VP, Wool = 4, Wine = 3, Cheese = 2, Grain = 1
Prioritize routes with high-value goods when moving your Merchant
Pay Grain first for Harvest events — cheapest to lose
Advancing further on Farmers Track yields better goods

summary.title: Build Trading Stations in Clusters
bullets: Ship/Wagon costs 3 followers per move — movement is expensive
Plan routes to build stations in connected towns, not scattered
Each station = VP equal to Development Status at game end
Most stations overall earns a bonus Citizen Tile

walkthrough.title: The Development Rush
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest strategies in Orleans focuses on pushing the Development Track as fast as possible. Your Development Status multiplies every Trading Station and Citizen Tile, so reaching Status 4 or 5 before opponents is a massive advantage. Start by recruiting a Craftsman for Technology, then a Scholar via the University. Use the Scriptorium if you acquire it. The Pharmacy Place Tile lets you spend coins for Development Points at will. Stack development early and your entire scoring structure benefits for the rest of the game. Every Citizen Tile and Trading Station you collect becomes worth more than your opponents' equivalents.

walkthrough.title: The Trading Station Network
walkthrough.text: Building many Trading Stations is the most reliable path to high scores when combined with strong Development Status. The Guildhall requires Craftsman, Trader, and Knight — three different types — so you need solid bag management. Plan your Merchant's route to hit 6-8 towns in sequence. Use both Ship and Wagon actions to maximize coverage. The Horse Wagon Place Tile is excellent here because it lets you move along Roads and collect goods in a single action with only two followers. Remember: the player with the most Trading Stations at game end receives a bonus Citizen Tile, which is also multiplied by Development Status.

walkthrough.title: Technology Tile Optimization
walkthrough.text: Technology Tiles are among the most powerful resources in Orleans because they permanently reduce your draw requirements. Your first tile must replace a Farmer, so place it on the action you use most often (Farm House or Village are ideal since both require a Farmer-type). Subsequent tiles can replace any type except Monks. Target high-frequency actions. The Laboratory Place Tile is exceptional because it gives you a Technology Tile with fewer restrictions. With 3-4 Technology Tiles placed, you can activate complex actions with just 1-2 drawn followers, freeing draws for other Places.

walkthrough.title: Monk Strategy
walkthrough.text: Monks are the wildcard of Orleans. They substitute for any follower type, making them incredibly flexible. The Monastery requires all four basic types (Farmer, Boatman, Craftsman, Trader), which is expensive, but a Monk in your bag means you can activate any Place that is one follower short. Two Monks and you can activate almost anything. The key tension is that Monks cannot be replaced by Technology Tiles and are blocked during Pilgrimage rounds (3 out of 18). Also, contributing Monks to Beneficial Deeds is not allowed since the Deeds require specific types. Use Monks as versatile gap-fillers rather than building your entire strategy around them.

walkthrough.title: Beneficial Deeds as Bag Thinning
walkthrough.text: Advanced players recognize that Beneficial Deeds serve a dual purpose: scoring and bag thinning. When you send a follower to the Deeds board, it leaves the game permanently. This removes a tile from your bag, making your draws more predictable. If you have duplicate Farmers or Boatmen clogging your bag, sending them to Deeds cleans up your draws while earning coins and eventually Citizen Tiles. The Canalization Deed is particularly valuable because it gives a choice of 1 coin or 1 Development Point per tile. Time your Deeds contributions to coincide with bag bloat rather than depleting key followers you still need.

walkthrough.title: Place Tile Tier List
walkthrough.text: Not all Place Tiles are equal. The strongest from Stack I: Bathhouse (draw 3 from bag, place 2 on Action Spaces — incredible action acceleration), Brewery (2 coins for 1 follower — efficient economy), and Horse Wagon (move along Road and collect good). From Stack II: Laboratory (gain Technology Tile with fewer restrictions), School (Scholars substitute for any non-Monk follower — amazing flexibility), and Pharmacy (spend coins for Development Points). The Office (1 coin per Trading Station) scales powerfully in a Trading Station strategy. Tailor Shop and Wool Manufacturer are solid for goods-focused play. The Sacristy protects against negative events and is clutch in the mid-to-late game when Harvest, Taxes, and Plague start hurting.

walkthrough.title: Goods Collection vs. Trading Stations
walkthrough.text: There are two main scoring engines in Orleans: goods collection and Trading Station networks. Goods are simpler — move your Merchant, collect high-value tiles, done. No multiplier needed. Brocade at 5 VP is pure profit. Trading Stations require Development investment but scale higher with Status multipliers. The optimal approach is usually a hybrid: collect goods along your Merchant's route (they are free when moving anyway) while building Trading Stations at each stop. Do not choose one path exclusively. Even a goods-focused player should build a few stations, and a station-focused player should never leave Brocade on the map uncollected.

walkthrough.title: Census Manipulation
walkthrough.text: The Census phase awards 1 coin to the Farmers Track leader and penalizes the trailer. Over 18 rounds, this can swing 18+ coins. Investing in Farmers early to grab the Census lead pays cumulative dividends. Even 1 Farmer ahead is enough if no one else invests. The trick is not to over-invest. Advancing on the Farmers Track also yields goods, so the first few advances are highly efficient. After collecting the best goods, the track's returns diminish. Let someone else contest the lead if they want to spend resources fighting for it. The 2-player variant removes the trailing penalty, making the Census lead less critical but still worth 1 free coin per round.

walkthrough.title: Citizen Tile Racing
walkthrough.text: There are 14 Citizen Tiles in the game, and each is multiplied by your Development Status at game end. Collecting even 3 Citizen Tiles with Status 4 is 12 VP — nearly as much as two Brocade tiles. Citizen Tiles come from: reaching the end of the Boatmen Track (1 tile), reaching the fourth Knight position (1 tile), reaching Development Track Citizen spaces (multiple), completing Beneficial Deeds (multiple), and having the most Trading Stations at game end (1 tile). These are first-come-first-served, so racing to them before opponents matters. Track what other players are pursuing and snipe tiles they are close to if the cost is reasonable.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Coin Management
walkthrough.text: Coins are worth 1 VP each at game end, which makes every coin meaningful in a tight game. In the late game, stop spending coins unless the return is clearly worth more than 1 VP. The Income event pays coins equal to your Development Status — with Status 4, that is 4 free coins (4 VP) each time Income appears. The Trading Day event pays 1 coin per Trading Station. A player with 6 stations collects 6 coins every Trading Day. Stack these repeating income sources and your coin pile grows automatically. Avoid Torture at all costs in the late game — losing a Trading Station or Development Point in the final rounds is devastating because you cannot recover.

summary.title: The Development Rush
bullets: Push Development Status to 4-5 before opponents
University + Scriptorium + Pharmacy for accelerated development
Development Status multiplies ALL Trading Stations and Citizen Tiles
Early investment compounds: every future station/citizen scores more

summary.title: The Trading Station Network
bullets: Target 6-8 Trading Stations with Guildhall (Craftsman + Trader + Knight)
Plan Merchant route for sequential town coverage
Horse Wagon Place Tile: move + collect goods with fewer followers
Most stations = bonus Citizen Tile (also multiplied by Development)

summary.title: Technology Tile Optimization
bullets: First tile replaces Farmer on your most-used Place (Farm House or Village)
Subsequent tiles: replace any type except Monks on high-frequency actions
Laboratory Place Tile grants Technology with fewer restrictions
3-4 Technology Tiles = activate complex Places with minimal draws

bullets: Monks substitute for any follower type — ultimate flexibility
Monastery costs all 4 basic types — expensive but powerful
Cannot be replaced by Technology; blocked during Pilgrimage (3 of 18 rounds)

bullets: Removing followers from bag permanently = more predictable draws
Send duplicates/excess followers to Deeds for coins + eventual Citizen Tiles

bullets: Farmers Track leader gets 1 coin/round — up to 18+ coins over the game
Early Farmer investment also yields goods from the track
Do not over-invest; let opponents contest the lead if they want to fight

entries.definition: One of the 7 follower types. Recruited via the Farm House (requires Boatman + Craftsman). Advancing on the Farmers Track yields goods and affects Census income/penalty. Used in many action requirements including Village, University, Castle, Monastery, and Wagon.

One of the 7 follower types. Recruited via the Village (requires Farmer + Boatman, choose Boatman option). Advancing on the Boatmen Track yields coins. The last space gives a Citizen Tile instead of coins to the first player there. Required for Farm House, Village, Castle, Monastery, and Ship.

One of the 7 follower types. Recruited via the Village (requires Farmer + Boatman, choose Craftsman option). Advancing on the Craftsmen Track yields a Technology Tile. Required for Farm House, Village, University, Monastery, Ship, and Guildhall.

One of the 7 follower types. Recruited via the Village (requires Farmer + Boatman, choose Trader option). Advancing on the Traders Track lets you gain a Place Tile to expand your city. Required for Village (as base), University, Castle, Monastery, and Guildhall.

One of the 7 follower types. Recruited via the Castle (requires Farmer + Boatman + Trader). Advancing on the Knights Track increases your follower draw limit from 4 up to 8. The fourth Knight position gives a Citizen Tile to the first player there. Required for Ship, Wagon, Guildhall, and Scriptorium.
One of the 7 follower types. Recruited via the University (requires Farmer + Craftsman + Trader). Advancing on the Scholars Track yields Development Points. Required for Wagon and Scriptorium.

One of the 7 follower types. Recruited via the Monastery (requires Farmer + Boatman + Craftsman + Trader). Has no track bonus. Monks are wildcards that substitute for any other follower type during planning. However, other tiles and Technology cannot substitute for Monks. Cannot be recruited during Pilgrimage rounds. Cannot be used for Beneficial Deeds.

Each player begins with 4 colored followers: 1 Farmer, 1 Boatman, 1 Craftsman, 1 Trader. These are marked in the player's color and have special protections: they cannot be lost to Plague, used for Beneficial Deeds, or sacrificed during Torture. They always remain with the player.
Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Boatman + Craftsman. Gain a Farmer tile, advance on Farmers Track, receive the depicted good. The Farmers Track position affects Census.

Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Farmer + Boatman. Choose one of three options: gain a Boatman (coins from track), gain a Craftsman (Technology Tile from track), or gain a Trader (Place Tile from track).
Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Farmer + Craftsman + Trader. Gain a Scholar tile and advance on the Scholars Track for Development Points.
Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Farmer + Boatman + Trader. Gain a Knight tile and advance on the Knights Track, increasing your follower draw limit.
Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Farmer + Boatman + Craftsman + Trader (all 4 basic types). Gain a Monk tile. No track bonus. Blocked during Pilgrimage event.

Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Boatman + Craftsman + Knight. Move your Merchant Token along a Waterway (blue connection) to an adjacent town. Collect one Goods Tile from the route if available.

Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Farmer + Knight + Scholar. Move your Merchant Token along a Road (brown connection) to an adjacent town. Collect one Goods Tile from the route if available.

Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Craftsman + Trader + Knight. Build a Trading Station in the town where your Merchant Token is present. One station per town (exception: Orleans allows one per player).
Action Place on the Player Board. Requires: Scholar + Knight. Gain 1 Development Point (advance on the Development Track).

Action Place on the Player Board. Place 1 or 2 Character Tiles during Planning (does not need all spaces filled). Action: move tiles to the Beneficial Deeds board for coins (1-3 per tile). Completing a Deed earns a Citizen Tile. Must use exact follower types shown; no Monks or starting colored followers.
Event (3 tiles in the Hour Glass deck). Players may not recruit Monks this round. Resolves during Phase 5 (Actions) rather than Phase 6 (Event). Round 1 always starts with Pilgrimage.
Event (3 tiles). Each player receives coins from the supply equal to their current Development Status. Higher Development Status means more income.
Event (3 tiles). Each player must pay 1 food item (Grain, Cheese, or Wine) back to the Goods Market. If unable, pay 5 coins instead. If unable to do that either, undergo Torture.
Event (3 tiles). Each player counts all their goods and pays 1 coin per 3 goods owned (rounded down). If unable to pay, undergo Torture.
Event (3 tiles). Each player receives 1 coin from the supply for each Trading Station they have built. Rewards early Trading Station investment.

Event (3 tiles). Each player draws 1 random Character Tile from their Followers Bag and returns it to the game board (it becomes available for recruitment again). Markers on tracks do not move back. If you draw a starting colored follower, you are lucky and lose nothing.

Triggered when a player cannot pay a required cost. For each missing coin, the player must sacrifice one item: a Trading Station, a Follower (random from bag, colored followers exempt), a Development Point (move backward on track, not past coin spaces), a Goods Tile, a Place Tile, or a Technology Tile. Items lost are removed from the game permanently. Only Development Points can be re-earned.
Goods type worth 1 VP at game end. There are 24 Grain tiles in the game. The cheapest good — pay this first during Harvest events. Collected from routes and the Farmers Track.
Goods type worth 2 VP at game end. There are 21 Cheese tiles in the game. Can be paid as food during Harvest events.
Goods type worth 3 VP at game end. There are 18 Wine tiles in the game. Can be paid as food during Harvest events.
Goods type worth 4 VP at game end. There are 15 Wool tiles in the game. Cannot be used to pay for Harvest — it is not a food item.
Goods type worth 5 VP at game end. There are 12 Brocade tiles in the game. The most valuable good. Cannot be used to pay for Harvest.

Located on the left side of the game board. Holds surplus Goods Tiles sorted by type. Production Place Tiles (Hayrick, Cheese Factory, etc.) take goods from here. Goods returned from Harvest events go back here.

Gained by advancing on the Craftsmen Track. Placed on an Action Space to permanently replace a follower requirement. First tile must replace a Farmer. Subsequent tiles replace any type except Monks. Max 1 per Place. Cannot be placed on single-follower Places. Cannot be moved after placement. Not removed after actions — permanent for the entire game. 16 total in the game.

Built via the Guildhall action. Each town can hold only one (exception: Orleans allows one per player). Worth VP equal to your Development Status at game end. Player with the most stations earns a bonus Citizen Tile. Each player has 10 stations. Can be lost to Torture.

Special tiles worth VP equal to your Development Status at game end. 14 total in the game. Earned from: end of Boatmen Track, fourth Knight position, Development Track spaces, completing Beneficial Deeds, and having the most Trading Stations. First-come-first-served for track/deed tiles.

Each player's cloth bag holding their Character Tiles. Tiles return here after completing actions. Tiles are drawn from the bag during Phase 3 (Followers). Players may look into their bag at any time but must shuffle afterward. Functions like a deck in a deckbuilder.

Each player's personal board showing the standard action Places: Farm House, Village, University, Castle, Monastery, Ship, Wagon, Guildhall, Scriptorium, and Town Hall. Also includes the Market where drawn followers are placed.

The spaces on your Player Board where drawn Character Tiles are placed during Phase 3. Tiles on the Market are available for planning. Unused tiles remain between rounds, taking up space and reducing how many new tiles you can draw.

Each player's wooden figure that moves along Roads and Waterways on the game board. Starts in Orleans. Used to collect Goods Tiles via Ship/Wagon actions and to build Trading Stations via the Guildhall.

18 tiles that track the game's rounds and determine events. One is drawn per round in Phase 1. The Pilgrimage tile is always on top (Round 1). Each tile shows one of 6 event types. When the 18th tile is drawn, that is the final round.

Track on the game board measuring each player's development progress. Advanced by earning Development Points (from Scholars Track, Scriptorium, Place Tiles). Contains coin spaces, Citizen Tile spaces, and Development Status spaces. Status starts at 1 and increases as you advance.

Your current development level as shown on the Development Track. Starts at 1. Determines: coins received from Income event, coins from Hospital Place Tile, and VP value of each Trading Station and Citizen Tile at game end. The most important scoring multiplier in the game.

Character track advanced by recruiting Farmers via the Farm House. Yields goods as you advance. Position determines Census income (leader gets 1 coin) and penalty (trailer pays 1 coin). Cannot recruit more Farmers once at the end of the track.
Character track advanced by recruiting Boatmen via the Village. Yields coins as you advance. The last space gives a Citizen Tile instead of coins to the first player who reaches it.
Character track advanced by recruiting Craftsmen via the Village. Yields a Technology Tile on each advance. Technology Tiles permanently fill Action Spaces.
Character track advanced by recruiting Traders via the Village. Each advance lets you gain a Place Tile to expand your city. First advance: Stack I only. Subsequent: Stack I or II.

Character track advanced by recruiting Knights via the Castle. Determines how many followers you may draw from your bag each round (starts at 4, max 8). The fourth position gives a Citizen Tile to the first player there.
Character track advanced by recruiting Scholars via the University. Yields Development Points on each advance, which move your marker on the Development Track.

Separate small board where players can send followers via the Town Hall action. Each space requires a specific follower type and pays 1-3 coins. Completing a Deed (filling all spaces) earns a Citizen Tile. Followers sent here are permanently removed from the game. Canalization Deed offers a choice of 1 coin or 1 Development Point.
Place Tile (Stack I). Receive 1 Grain from the Goods Market. Cannot play if no Grain is available.
Place Tile (Stack I). Receive 1 Cheese from the Goods Market. Cannot play if no Cheese is available.
Place Tile (Stack I). Receive 1 Wine from the Goods Market. Cannot play if no Wine is available.
Place Tile (Stack I). Receive 1 Wool from the Goods Market. Cannot play if no Wool is available.
Place Tile. Receive 1 Brocade from the Goods Market. Cannot play if no Brocade is available.
Place Tile. Receive 1 Development Point (advance on the Development Track).
Place Tile. Receive 2 coins from the supply. Simple and efficient economy action.
Place Tile. Receive 2 Development Points (advance 2 spaces on the Development Track).
Place Tile. Receive 2 coins from the supply and 1 Development Point.
Place Tile. Receive 4 coins from the supply. Strong economy action.
Place Tile. Activated by any single Character Tile. Spend 1-3 coins to advance 1-3 spaces on the Development Track. Excellent for targeted development pushes.
Place Tile. Receive 1 coin for each Trading Station you have built. Scales with Trading Station count.

Place Tile. Draw 3 Character Tiles from your bag. Choose 2 and immediately place them on Action Spaces (not the Bathhouse). The third tile and the Bathhouse's activating follower return to your bag. Exceptional action acceleration.
Place Tile. Receive coins from the supply equal to your current Development Status. Scales with development progress.
Place Tile. As the owner, you can use Scholars in place of any Character Tile type except Monks. Incredible flexibility — Scholars become wildcards similar to Monks.
Place Tile. Move your Merchant Token along a Road to an adjacent town and collect one good if available. Like the Wagon action but may require fewer followers.
Place Tile. As the owner, you can use Boatmen in place of Craftsmen, Traders, and Farmers. Gives Boatmen wildcard versatility for those three types.

Place Tile. Activated by a Monk. During the Event Phase, return the Monk to your bag instead of suffering the event's negative effects (Harvest, Taxes, Plague). Does not protect against positive events (Income, Trading Day) since those are beneficial.

Place Tile. Expands your Market by 2 spaces. During Phase 3 (Followers), place 1-2 Character Tiles on the Gunpowder Tower. During Planning, place them on Action Spaces. During Actions, use them for Beneficial Deeds. Starting colored followers cannot be sent to Beneficial Deeds.
Place Tile. Gain a Technology Tile that you may place on any Action Space (except Monk spaces). Standard Technology rules apply. The Technology Tile may also be placed on the Laboratory itself.
The first tier of Place Tiles. Available from your first Traders Track advance. Contains simpler production and economy tiles.
The second tier of Place Tiles. Available from your second Traders Track advance onward (may also choose from Stack I). Contains more powerful and specialized tiles.

Phase 2 of each round. Checks the Farmers Track. Player furthest ahead receives 1 coin. Player furthest behind pays 1 coin. Ties cancel the reward/penalty. In 2-player games, the trailing player does not pay.

Phase 4 of each round. Players simultaneously place Character Tiles from their Market onto Action Spaces to activate Places. Can spread activation across multiple rounds. All players plan at the same time, then announce completion.

Phase 5 of each round. Starting with Start Player, clockwise: each player takes 1 activated action or passes. Passing ends your participation. After an action, Character Tiles return to bag (Technology stays). Unused activated actions carry over. Phase ends when all players pass.
Given to the youngest player at game start. Passes to the left at the end of each round (Phase 7). The Start Player draws the Hour Glass Tile and takes the first action each round.

Recommended for experienced players. Before the first round, each player may exclude 1 Place Tile (Stack I or II) from the game. In a 2-player game, each player excludes 2 tiles. Removed tiles go back to the box.
At any time during the game, you may look into your Followers Bag to see which and how many followers are inside. After looking, shuffle the tiles so you cannot draw specific tiles on purpose.

Character, Technology, and Goods Tiles are limited. You cannot gain a tile that is unavailable. When tiles run out, corresponding actions cannot be played. Events (Harvest, Plague) may return tiles to availability. Coins may temporarily run out, preventing coin rewards.

You cannot move a Character Tile from one Action Space to another. However, during Phase 3 (Followers), for each draw you decline, you may move 1 tile from an Action Space back to your Market. This lets you rearrange your plans.
Once your marker reaches the last space of any Character Track, you may no longer take the corresponding recruitment action. You cannot advance further.
